questions.text: Which of the following will react with water?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Does NOT react with water under normal conditions.It is slightly soluble in water but does not undergo hydrolysis.
Aldehydes react with water to form hydrates.Trichloroacetaldehyde undergoes hydration in water to form chloral hydrate:
Insoluble and unreactive with water.The C-Cl bonds in CCl₄ are very stable due to the symmetrical tetrahedral structure, making it resistant to hydrolysis.
Does NOT react with water under normal conditions.The C-Cl bonds in alkyl halides are not easily hydrolyzed unless strong acids, bases, or heat are applied.

questions.explanation: Cl3CCHO will react with water. Water is a polar solvent. Like dissolves like. A polar solvent will be dissolved in a polar and a non-polar compound will be dissolved in a nonpolar solvent. CCl4, ClCH2CH2Cl are non-polar. So they will not be able to dissolve in water. Cl3CCOOH will form hydrogen bonds with water due to which it will dissolve in water. Such hydrogen bonding is not present in the case of CHCl3. Chloral (Cl3CCHO) reacts with water to form chloral hydrate, CCl3CH(OH)2

questions.text: Many elements have fractional atomic masses. This is because:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.text: Mass of atom is itself reactional
options.explanation: The mass of an atom is not reactional. There is no such mass to be said as reactional. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Atomic masses are average masses of isobars
options.explanation: Isobars are atoms that have the same mass number but a different number of protons. The fractional atomic mass of an element is not due to the average masses of isobars. But due to the average masses of its isotopes.

options.text: Atomic masses are average masses of isotopes
options.explanation: Isotopes are the atoms having same proton but different neutron numbers. The fractional atomic mass of an element is not due to the average masses of isotopes. But due to the average masses of its isotopes.

options.text: Atomic masses are average masses of isotopes proportional to their abundance
options.explanation: Atomic masses are typically reported as weighted averages that take into account the abundance of each isotope of an element. Most elements exist as a mixture of isotopes, and these isotopes have different masses. The atomic mass of an element listed on the periodic table is a weighted average of the masses of its naturally occurring isotopes. The weighting is based on the abundance of each isotope in nature. Since isotopes have different masses, the atomic mass is not a whole number, and it may appear as a fractional value.

questions.text: In which of the following will water evaporate earlier?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The rate of water evaporation depends on various factors such as the surface area exposed to air, temperature, humidity, and airflow. In general, water will evaporate more quickly in conditions where these factors promote evaporation. If we assume that the initial conditions (temperature, humidity, etc.) are the same for all containers, then the primary factor influencing the rate of evaporation would be the surface area exposed to air. Larger surface areas allow for more water molecules to be in contact with the air, promoting faster evaporation. A wide saucepan typically has a larger surface area compared to a cup, glass, or small bowl. Therefore, under similar conditions, water is likely to evaporate earlier from a saucepan than from the other containers.

questions.text: Different kinds of atoms of the same element are called isotopes having different _ but same _ properties.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect.Different kinds of atoms of the same element, which have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons (resulting in different masses), are called isotopes. Isotopes of the same element have different physical properties, such as different atomic masses and, in some cases, different nuclear stabilities. However, isotopes of the same element have the same chemical properties. This is because chemical properties are primarily determined by the number and arrangement of electrons, which is determined by the number of protons in the nucleus. Since isotopes of the same element have the same number of protons, they exhibit the same chemical behavior.

questions.text: The unit used to express the relative atomic mass is called?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The gram (originally gramme; SI unit symbol g) is a unit of mass in the International System of Units (SI) equal to one one thousandth of a kilogram.

Avogadro's number, number of units in one mole of any substance (defined as its molecular weight in grams), equal to 6.02214076 × 1023. The units may be electrons, atoms, ions, or molecules, depending on the nature of the substance and the character of the reaction (if any).
The quantity of matter contained in an atom of an element is atomic mass.

One atomic mass unit (1u) is a mass unit equal to exactly one-twelfth (1/12th) the mass of one atom of carbon-12 isotope, which is the scale used to compare atomic masses. So, the unit used to express relative atomic mass is amu.

questions.explanation: One atomic mass unit (1amu) is a mass unit equal to exactly one-twelfth (1/12th) the mass of one atom of carbon-12 isotope, which is the scale used to compare atomic masses. So, the unit used to express relative atomic mass is amu. Thus, option D is correct.

questions.text: How many molecules are there in 2.1 moles of CO2:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Since there are 2.1 moles of CO2, we can easily find out the number of molecules present in it by multiplying 2.1 with the Avagadro's constant, which is 6.02 × 10²³. Hence, the answer would be approximately 1.2 x 10²⁴. The answer choice that is closest to the obtained value is 1.26 x 10²⁴. Please do not calculate the total number of atoms in this question; you have only been asked to calculate the total number of molecules.

options.explanation: 2g of H equals two moles. Each mole of H atoms has 1 mole of electrons only, so two moles of H have twice the number of electrons than Avogadro's number.

Avogadro's Number (NA) is the number of atoms, molecules, ions, or anything in one mole of the respective substance. As 6.02 x 1023 atoms, molecules, ions, or electrons are present in one mole; therefore, this number is known as Avogadro's number.Only 24g of Mg equals one mole of Mg. Thus, only 24g of Mg has Avogadro's number of atoms.
1g of He equals 1/2 mole of He. Thus it has half the number of atoms than Avogadro's number.
35.5g of chlorine equals 1/2 mole of chlorine gas. Thus, it has half the number of molecules than Avogadro's number.

questions.text: Natural gas in homes burns in excess of oxygen, what is the limiting reactant in this case?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The question states that the gas burns in excess oxygen, meaning oxygen is available in surplus and is not the limiting reactant.

The limiting reactant is the substance that gets completely consumed first, stopping the reaction from continuing. Since oxygen is in excess, the natural gas (primarily methane, CH₄) is the limiting reactant because it determines how much combustion occurs.
Air is a mixture of gases (mostly nitrogen and oxygen), and oxygen is already provided in excess, so air as a whole is not a limiting factor.
Incorrect. In any reaction, there must be a limiting reactant if one reactant is present in excess. In this case, natural gas is the limiting reactant.

questions.explanation: The limiting reactant is the substance that gets completely consumed first, stopping the reaction from continuing. Since oxygen is in excess, the natural gas (primarily methane, CH₄) is the limiting reactant because it determines how much combustion occurs.

questions.text: Spectrometry is used when reactants and products absorb _.

options.explanation: This option is correct. Spectrometry is used when reactants and products absorb ultraviolet (UV) radiations. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectrometry is a common technique used to analyze the absorption of UV or visible light by chemical substances.

This option is also correct. Spectrometry can be used when reactants and products absorb visible light. UV-Vis spectrometry covers both ultraviolet and visible regions of the electromagnetic spectrum.

This option is not correct. Infrared (IR) spectrometry is used to study the vibrations of chemical bonds in molecules and is primarily used for identifying functional groups in organic compounds, rather than measuring absorbance of reactants and products.

This option is correct. Spectrometry can be used for analyzing the absorption of UV, visible, and infrared radiations by different chemical substances, depending on the specific technique and the properties of the molecules being studied.

questions.text: Limiting reactant controls the amount of _ ?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The reactants are the substances that are present at the start of the reaction. They are converted into products by the reaction. The limiting reactant is a reactant, but it is not the only reactant.

The limiting reactant, also known as the limiting reagent, is the reactant that is completely consumed or exhausted first in a chemical reaction. It determines the maximum amount of product that can be formed in the reaction.The limiting reactant controls the amount of products that can be produced because once it is consumed, the reaction cannot proceed further. The other reactants may still be present, but they cannot participate in the reaction without the limiting reactant.
This option is incorrect because the limiting reactant only controls the amount of products formed. It does not control the amount of reactants.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: he limiting reactant controls the amount of products formed in a chemical reaction. The limiting reactant is the reactant that is completely consumed first, and it determines the maximum amount of product that can be formed. The other reactants, called excess reactants, are not completely consumed and are left over at the end of the reaction.So the answer is products.Here is an example. Let's say we have the following chemical reaction:2H2+ O2 → 2H2OThis reaction requires 2 moles of hydrogen gas for every 1 mole of oxygen gas. If we start with 1 mole of hydrogen gas and 2 moles of oxygen gas, then the hydrogen gas will be the limiting reactant. This is because the hydrogen gas will be completely consumed first, and only 1 mole of water will be formed. The oxygen gas will be left over in excess

questions.text: The total number of neutrons in 5g of H2O are:1 NA = 6.023 x 1023
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: This option incorrectly assumes a much higher number of neutrons than actually present in 5g of H2O. The calculation does not align with the molecular composition of water.
This option underestimates the total number of neutrons in 5g of H2O. The calculation method used is not correct according to the stoichiometric principles.

This is the correct option. It accurately calculates the total number of neutrons in 5g of H2O by first determining the number of moles and then using the molecular formula to find the total neutrons.
This option significantly underestimates the number of neutrons. The calculations do not reflect the correct stoichiometric relationships in water.

questions.explanation: To find the total number of neutrons in 5g of H2O, we start by calculating the number of moles of water:1. The molar mass of H2O is approximately 18 g/mol (2 for H and 16 for O).2. The number of moles in 5g of H2O is calculated as follows: Number of moles = mass (g) / molar mass (g/mol) = 5g / 18 g/mol ≈ 0.278 moles.3. Each molecule of H2O contains 2 hydrogen atoms (each with 0 neutrons) and 1 oxygen atom (with 8 neutrons), totaling 8 neutrons per molecule.4. The total number of molecules in 0.278 moles is: 0.278 moles x 6.023 x 1023 molecules/mole ≈ 1.67 x 1023 molecules.5. Therefore, the total number of neutrons in 5g of H2O is: 1.67 x 1023 molecules x 8 neutrons/molecule = 1.34 x 1024 neutrons, which corresponds to Option C. The other options are incorrect due to miscalculations or misunderstandings of stoichiometric principles.

questions.text: 2 moles of octane (C8H18) burn with 25 moles of oxygen (O2) and produced _ moles of carbon dioxide along with 18 moles of water.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Option (a) "14 moles" is not the correct answer. According to the balanced equation, only 8 moles of carbon dioxide are produced when 2 moles of octane react with oxygen.

Since we know that 18 moles of water are produced alongside carbon dioxide. The correct option must be the one which balances the equation properly. The balanced equation for combustion of octane is:2 C8H18 + 25 O2 —> 16 CO2 + 18 H2OThus, the correct option is B.
Option (c) "18 moles" is not the correct answer. The balanced equation indicates that 2 moles of octane produce 8 moles of carbon dioxide and 9 moles of water.
Option (d) "2 moles" is not the correct answer. The question states that 2 moles of octane are reacting, so this cannot be the number of moles of carbon dioxide produced.

questions.text: Formula mass is considered for _ compounds instead of their molecular mass:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: "Metallic" is incorrect because metallic compounds are composed of metal atoms bonded together by metallic bonds, not ionic or covalent bonds.
The correct answer is "Ionic" Formula mass is considered for compounds instead of molecular mass for ionic compounds.
"Covalent" is incorrect because covalent compounds are formed by the sharing of electrons between atoms, and the concept of formula mass is not typically used for covalent compounds.
"Polar covalent" is incorrect because polar covalent compounds also involve the sharing of electrons between atoms, and the concept of formula mass is not typically used for these compounds either.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is "Ionic" Formula mass is considered for compounds instead of molecular mass for ionic compounds.

questions.text: Molecular ions are produced by passing a high-energy electron beam or an x-ray beam through:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Atoms: When x-rays pass through atoms, they can cause the ionization of individual atoms, resulting in the formation of atomic ions. However, the question specifically mentions molecular ions, which are formed from molecules rather than individual atoms. So, this option is incorrect.
Ions: X-rays do not typically produce molecular ions when passing through pre-existing ions.

The correct answer is "gas," specifically a gaseous substance. When x-rays are passed through a gas, such as air or a gas sample, they can cause the ionization of atoms within the gas. This ionization process occurs when the x-rays transfer enough energy to the gas atoms, causing them to lose one or more electrons and form positively charged ions. The resulting ions are called molecular ions because they are formed from molecules in the gas.

Solid: X-rays passing through a solid material primarily interact with the atoms within the solid. The ionization of atoms can occur, leading to the formation of atomic ions, but the question specifically asks for molecular ions

questions.explanation: The ion that is produced when a molecule loses or gains one or more electrons is called a molecular ion, e.g., CH4+, CO+, or N2+. Cationic molecular ions are more abundant than anionic molecular ions. When a high-energy electron beam or an X-ray beam passes through a gas, it can produce molecular ions. The high-energy particles, either electrons or X-ray photons, interact with the gas molecules, resulting in ionization. In the case of electron ionization, a high-energy electron beam is directed at a gaseous sample. The electrons collide with the gas molecules, leading to the removal of one or more electrons from the molecules.

questions.text: Glucose(C6H12O6) is the most important nutrient in a cell for generating chemical potential energy, what is the mass percent of carbon in 1.5g of sample?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The mass percent of the carbon in a 1.5g sample of glucose is approximately 40%. So, it is incorrect.

To determine the mass percent of carbon in a sample of glucose (C6H12O6), we need to calculate the mass of carbon in the sample and then express it as a percentage of the total mass. The molar mass of glucose is calculated as follows: C: 6 atoms x 12.01 g/mol = 72.06 g/mol H: 12 atoms x 1.01 g/mol = 12.12 g/mol O: 6 atoms x 16.00 g/mol = 96.00 g/mol Total molar mass of glucose: 72.06 g/mol + 12.12 g/mol + 96.00 g/mol = 180.18 g/mol Now, to find the mass of carbon in 1.5 g of glucose, we can use the molar mass ratio of carbon to glucose: Mass of carbon = (Mass of glucose / Molar mass of glucose) x Molar mass of carbon Mass of carbon = (1.5 g / 180.18 g/mol) x 72.06 g/mol Mass of carbon = 0.599 g Finally, we can calculate the mass percent of carbon in the sample: Mass percent of carbon = (Mass of carbon / Mass of sample) x 100 Mass percent of carbon = (0.599 g / 1.5 g) x 100 Mass percent of carbon = 39.93% Therefore, the mass percent of carbon in a 1.5 g sample of glucose is approximately 39.93%.
The mass percent of the carbon in a 1.5g sample of glucose is approximately 40%. So, it is incorrect.
The mass percent of the carbon in a 1.5g sample of glucose is approximately 40%. So, it is incorrect.

The molar mass of glucose is calculated as follows: C: 6 atoms x 12.01 g/mol = 72.06 g/mol H: 12 atoms x 1.01 g/mol = 12.12 g/mol O: 6 atoms x 16.00 g/mol = 96.00 g/mol Total molar mass of glucose: 72.06 g/mol + 12.12 g/mol + 96.00 g/mol = 180.18 g/mol Now, to find the mass of carbon in 1.5 g of glucose, we can use the molar mass ratio of carbon to glucose: Mass of carbon = (Mass of glucose / Molar mass of glucose) x Molar mass of carbon Mass of carbon = (1.5 g / 180.18 g/mol) x 72.06 g/mol Mass of carbon = 0.599 g Finally, we can calculate the mass percent of carbon in the sample: Mass percent of carbon = (Mass of carbon / Mass of sample) x 100 Mass percent of carbon = (0.599 g / 1.5 g) x 100 Mass percent of carbon = 39.93% Therefore, the mass percent of carbon in a 1.5 g sample of glucose is approximately 39.93%.

questions.text: The break down of molecular ions from natural products give important information about?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The breakdown of molecular ions does not directly provide information about the size of the natural product. Size is typically determined by other techniques such as mass spectrometry or molecular weight determination.

The position of specific atoms or functional groups within the natural product is not directly revealed through the breakdown of molecular ions. Methods like NMR spectroscopy are used for this purpose.

The shape of a natural product's structure is not directly deduced from the molecular ion breakdown. Structural information requires more detailed analysis, such as X-ray crystallography or spectroscopic methods.

D is correct as in “Mass Spectrometry”, large molecules are broken down into smaller unstable ions in the Ionization Chamber. These ions are then separated according to their mass to charge ratio by accelerating them and passing them through an electrical or a magnetic field. They are then detected by a detector. Results are displayed as a Spectra. The data is then correlated with atoms or molecules having known mass.

questions.text: Which of the following statement about Avogadro's hypothesis is correct?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.text: Under similar conditions of temperature and pressure, gases react with each other in simple ratio.
options.explanation: Gases do not necessarily react with each other in simple ratios. While Avogadro's hypothesis states that equal volumes of gases contain the same number of particles, this does not mean that gases will react with each other in simple ratios. The ratio in which gases react depends on the specific chemical reaction taking place, as well as the temperature, pressure, and other conditions. In some cases, gases may react in simple ratios, while in other cases, the ratios may be more complex.

options.text: Under similar conditions of temperature and pressure, equal volumes of all gases contain same number of molecules.
options.explanation: Avogadro's hypothesis states that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain the same number of particles, regardless of their chemical nature and physical properties. This hypothesis is the basis for the concept of the mole, which is a unit of measurement used in chemistry to express the amount of a substance. One mole of a substance is defined as the amount of that substance that contains the same number of particles as there are atoms in exactly 12 grams of carbon-12.

options.text: At NTP all gases contain same number of molecules.
options.explanation: Gases do not necessarily contain the same number of molecules at NTP (normal temperature and pressure). While gases at NTP have a standard temperature of 273.15 K (0 °C) and a standard pressure of 1 atmosphere (101.325 kPa), the number of molecules in a gas sample depends on the volume of the sample, the temperature, the pressure, and the number of moles of gas present. According to Avogadro's hypothesis, equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain the same number of particles, but this does not necessarily mean that all gases contain the same number of molecules at NTP. The number of molecules in a gas sample can be calculated using the ideal gas law, which takes into account the volume, temperature, pressure, and number of moles of gas present.

options.text: Gases always react with gases only at given temperature and pressure.
options.explanation: Gases can react with other types of substances besides gases, such as liquids or solids. The ability of gases to react with other substances depends on the specific chemical reaction taking place, as well as the temperature, pressure, and other conditions. While gases are more likely to react with other gases under certain conditions, such as at high temperatures and pressures, this is not always the case. For example, many chemical reactions involving gases take place in the presence of a catalyst, which can help to facilitate the reaction between the gas and another substance.

questions.explanation: Avogadro's hypothesis states that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain the same number of particles, regardless of their chemical nature and physical properties. This hypothesis is the basis for the concept of the mole, which is a unit of measurement used in chemistry to express the amount of a substance. One mole of a substance is defined as the amount of that substance that contains the same number of particles as there are atoms in exactly 12 grams of carbon-12.

questions.text: Chemical equations do not tell about the _ because of certain limitations:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Rate of reaction: Chemical equations show what reacts and what is produced, but not how fast the reaction occurs. Kinetics, a separate area of chemistry, deals with reaction rates.

options.text: Physical states of reactants and products.
options.explanation: Physical states of reactants and products: While sometimes state symbols (like (s), (l), (g), and (aq)) are included, they are not always present. A basic chemical equation doesn't always specify if substances are solid, liquid, gas, or aqueous.

Conditions: Chemical equations often omit specific conditions like temperature, pressure, or the presence of catalysts, which are crucial for many reactions. While conditions are sometimes written above or below the reaction arrow, it is not always done.

Chemical equations involve the elements involved in a reaction, the number of molecules of the elements, the state symbols. It has specific limitations and does not show the rate of reaction and condition required for reaction to occur. Chemical equations do not give information about physical states of reactants and products. The terms like pressure, temperature and volume also affect the chemical reaction. The rate of a reaction cannot be known by looking at chemical reactions.

questions.explanation: Chemical equations involve the elements involved in a reaction, the number of molecules of the elements, the state symbols. It has specific limitations and does not show the rate of reaction and condition required for reaction to occur. Chemical equations give information about physical states of reactants and products. The terms like pressure, temperature and volume also affect the chemical reaction. The rate of a reaction cannot be known by looking at chemical reactions

questions.text: Atomicity is determined by the number of _ present in a molecule.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Fundamental Principles of Organic Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is not directly related to atomicity. Dots are often used in structural formulas or Lewis dot diagrams to represent the valence electrons of atoms, but they do not determine the atomicity of a molecule.

Atomicity refers to the number of atoms bonded together to form a molecule. The number of atoms present in a molecule determines its atomicity. For example, a molecule of oxygen gas (O2) has an atomicity of 2 because it consists of two oxygen atoms bonded together.

Sub particles generally refer to particles that make up atoms, such as protons, neutrons, and electrons. While these sub particles contribute to the overall structure of atoms, they do not directly determine the atomicity of a molecule.

Electrons are negatively charged subatomic particles that orbit around the nucleus of an atom. While electrons play a crucial role in chemical bonding, they alone do not determine the atomicity of a molecule. The atomicity is determined by the number of atoms present in a molecule.

questions.text: The mass of one mole of electron is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

Mass spectrometers measure the relative abundances of isotopes of an element. Mass spectrometers are analytical instruments used to measure the mass-to-charge ratio of ions. One of the key applications of mass spectrometry is determining the relative abundances of isotopes in a sample. The basic principle involves ionizing a sample, then separating and detecting ions based on their mass-to-charge ratio. Since isotopes of an element have different masses, the mass spectrometer can distinguish between them, allowing scientists to determine the relative abundance of each isotope in a given sample.

questions.text: 30 grams of 2-propanol were mixed with excess acidified K2Cr2O7 and boiled under reflux for 20 minutes. The organic product was then collected by distillation. The yield of product was 75.0%. What is the mass of product produced?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: According to given data, this option is incorrect.

To calculate the mass of the product produced, you can use the yield percentage. The yield percentage is calculated as follows: Yield (%) = Actual Yield / Theoretical Yield × 100 In this case, the yield is given as 75.0%, and mass of 2-propanol is 30 grams. The molecular formula of 2-propanol (isopropanol) is C3H8O. Molar mass of C3H8O = (3 * 12.01 g/mol) + (8 * 1.01 g/mol) + (1 * 16.00 g/mol) = 60.03 g/molTheoretical Yield = (Mass of 2-propanol) × (Theoretical Yield / Molar Mass of 2-propanol) Theoretical Yield = (30 g) × (1 mol / 60.03 g) = 0.4999 mol Now, calculate the actual yield using the given yield percentage: Yield (%) = (Actual Yield / Theoretical Yield) × 100 75.0% = (Actual Yield / 0.4999 mol) × 100 Actual Yield = (75.0% / 100) × 0.4999 mol = 0.3749 mol Now, convert the actual yield from moles to grams using the molar mass of the product you expect to obtain. Actual Yield (grams) = 0.3749 mol × 60.03 g/mol = 22.51 g So, the mass of the product produced is approximately 22.51 grams, which is closest to option (b) 21.75g.
According to given data, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: Which one is not true about isotope?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: This statement is not true. Isotopes are defined by having the same number of protons (which determines the element) but different numbers of neutrons. This difference in neutron count leads to variations in atomic mass among isotopes.

This statement is not true. The mass number is the sum of protons and neutrons in an atom's nucleus. Isotopes have different numbers of neutrons, so their mass numbers are not the same. The variation in mass number contributes to differences in atomic mass between isotopes.

This statement is not entirely true. While isotopes of an element do have similar chemical properties due to the same number of protons (which determines the element's behavior in chemical reactions), they can have slightly different physical properties due to their varying atomic masses. For example, the different atomic masses can influence properties like density and boiling point. However, the differences are often subtle and might not be noticeable under typical conditions.

This statement is true in the sense that options A, B, and C are all not true about isotopes. Isotopes do not have the same number of neutrons (option A), they do not have the same mass number (option B), and while their chemical properties are similar due to identical electronic configurations, they may exhibit slightly different physical properties (option C) due to differences in atomic mass.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D) "All of these." Isotopes have different numbers of neutrons, different mass numbers, and while they have similar chemical properties, they can have slight variations in physical properties due to differences in atomic mass.

questions.text: Indicate the incorrect statement from the following:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.text: A limiting reactant is consumed at the end of a reaction.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. A limiting reactant is completely consumed in a chemical reaction, determining the maximum amount of product formed.

options.text: Theoretical yield is always lower than actual yield.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Theoretical yield is the maximum amount of product that can be obtained from a reaction, assuming perfect conditions. Actual yield is often lower due to various practical losses.

options.text: Stoichiometric calculations can only be done if no side reaction happens.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. as it is the general rule and requirement for the calculation

options.text: The empirical formula and molecular formula of some compounds are the same.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. For some compounds, such as H2O, both the empirical and molecular formulas are identical.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B. Theoretical yield is the calculated maximum amount of product that can be produced in a chemical reaction under ideal conditions, whereas actual yield is the measured amount obtained from the experiment, which is often less due to factors such as incomplete reactions or losses during recovery. Options A, C, and D are correct statements: a limiting reactant is entirely consumed, stoichiometric calculations can be performed despite side reactions, and some compounds indeed have the same empirical and molecular formulas.

questions.text: 20 ml of a H3PO4 solution needs 40 ml of 0.1 M NaOH to convert it into sodium dihydrogen phosphate. How much volume of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 is needed to neutralize the same volume of the same H3PO4 completely?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Stoichiometry

options.explanation: Since 60 ml of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 is needed to completely neutralize the same volume of the given H3PO4 solution. Hence this option is incorrect.
Since 60 ml of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 is needed to completely neutralize the same volume of the given H3PO4 solution. Hence this option is incorrect.
Since 60 ml of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 is needed to completely neutralize the same volume of the given H3PO4 solution. Hence this option is incorrect.

To determine the volume of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 needed to neutralize the same volume of the given H3PO4 solution, we can use the concept of stoichiometry and the balanced chemical equation for the neutralization reaction.The balanced chemical equation for the neutralization reaction between H3PO4 and Ca(OH)2 is as follows:H3PO4 + 3 Ca(OH)2 → Ca(H2PO4)2 + 2 H2OFrom the equation, we can see that 1 mole of H3PO4 reacts with 3 moles of Ca(OH)2 to form 1 mole of Ca(H2PO4)2 and 2 moles of water.Given that 20 ml of H3PO4 solution reacts with 40 ml of 0.1 M NaOH, we can calculate the number of moles of H3PO4 reacting:Number of moles of H3PO4 = Volume of H3PO4 solution (in L) × Molarity of H3PO4Number of moles of H3PO4 = 20 ml × (1 L / 1000 ml) × 0.1 mol/LNumber of moles of H3PO4 = 0.002 molesSince 1 mole of H3PO4 reacts with 3 moles of Ca(OH)2, the number of moles of Ca(OH)2 needed to react completely with the given H3PO4 is:Number of moles of Ca(OH)2 = Number of moles of H3PO4 × (3 moles of Ca(OH)2 / 1 mole of H3PO4)Number of moles of Ca(OH)2 = 0.002 moles × 3Number of moles of Ca(OH)2 = 0.006 molesNow, let's calculate the volume of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 required to provide 0.006 moles:Volume of Ca(OH)2 solution (in L) = Number of moles of Ca(OH)2 / Molarity of Ca(OH)2Volume of Ca(OH)2 solution (in L) = 0.006 moles / 0.1 mol/LVolume of Ca(OH)2 solution (in L) = 0.06 LFinally, convert the volume to milliliters (ml):Volume of Ca(OH)2 solution = 0.06 L × 1000 ml/LVolume of Ca(OH)2 solution = 60 mlSo, 60 ml of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 is needed to completely neutralize the same volume of the given H3PO4 solution.

questions.explanation: To determine the volume of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 needed to neutralize the same volume of the given H3PO4 solution, we can use the concept of stoichiometry and the balanced chemical equation for the neutralization reaction.The balanced chemical equation for the neutralization reaction between H3PO4 and Ca(OH)2 is as follows:H3PO4 + 3 Ca(OH)2 → Ca(H2PO4)2 + 2 H2OFrom the equation, we can see that 1 mole of H3PO4 reacts with 3 moles of Ca(OH)2 to form 1 mole of Ca(H2PO4)2 and 2 moles of water.Given that 20 ml of H3PO4 solution reacts with 40 ml of 0.1 M NaOH, we can calculate the number of moles of H3PO4 reacting:Number of moles of H3PO4 = Volume of H3PO4 solution (in L) × Molarity of H3PO4Number of moles of H3PO4 = 20 ml × (1 L / 1000 ml) × 0.1 mol/LNumber of moles of H3PO4 = 0.002 molesSince 1 mole of H3PO4 reacts with 3 moles of Ca(OH)2, the number of moles of Ca(OH)2 needed to react completely with the given H3PO4 is:Number of moles of Ca(OH)2 = Number of moles of H3PO4 × (3 moles of Ca(OH)2 / 1 mole of H3PO4)Number of moles of Ca(OH)2 = 0.002 moles × 3Number of moles of Ca(OH)2 = 0.006 molesNow, let's calculate the volume of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 required to provide 0.006 moles:Volume of Ca(OH)2 solution (in L) = Number of moles of Ca(OH)2 / Molarity of Ca(OH)2Volume of Ca(OH)2 solution (in L) = 0.006 moles / 0.1 mol/LVolume of Ca(OH)2 solution (in L) = 0.06 LFinally, convert the volume to milliliters (ml):Volume of Ca(OH)2 solution = 0.06 L × 1000 ml/LVolume of Ca(OH)2 solution = 60 mlSo, 60 ml of 0.1 M Ca(OH)2 is needed to completely neutralize the same volume of the given H3PO4 solution.

questions.text: When a 3.0 litre solution of normality N is mixed with 5.0 liter of 4M HCl, then the resultant solution has the normality 10. Find the value of N.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: If N were 10, then the number of moles of solute in the initial solution would be 10 x 3.0 = 30. However, in the resultant solution, it would be 10 x (3.0 + 5.0) = 80, which does not match the number of moles obtained by mixing the two solutions.

Normality (N) is given by the formula: N = (Number of moles of solute) / (Volume of solution in liters) Let's find the number of moles of solute in both solutions before and after mixing. Initial solution (3.0 liters of normality N): Number of moles of solute in the initial solution = N x 3.0 5.0 liters of 4M HCl solution: Number of moles of solute in the 4M HCl solution = 4 x 5.0 Resultant solution (mixture of both solutions with normality 10): Number of moles of solute in the resultant solution = 10 x (3.0 + 5.0) According to the problem, the resultant solution has the normality of 10, so we equate the moles of solute in the resultant solution with the calculated value: 10 x (3.0 + 5.0) = N x 3.0 + 4 x 5.0 Now, we solve for N: 10 x 8.0 = N x 3.0 + 20 80 = 3N + 20 3N = 60 N = 20

If N were 15, then the number of moles of solute in the initial solution would be 15 x 3.0 = 45. However, in the resultant solution, it would be 10 x (3.0 + 5.0) = 80, which does not match the number of moles obtained by mixing the two solutions.

If N were 25, then the number of moles of solute in the initial solution would be 25 x 3.0 = 75. However, in the resultant solution, it would be 10 x (3.0 + 5.0) = 80, which does not match the number of moles obtained by mixing the two solutions.

questions.explanation: Normality (N) is given by the formula: N = (Number of moles of solute) / (Volume of solution in liters) Let's find the number of moles of solute in both solutions before and after mixing. Initial solution (3.0 liters of normality N): Number of moles of solute in the initial solution = N x 3.0 5.0 liters of 4M HCl solution: Number of moles of solute in the 4M HCl solution = 4 x 5.0 Resultant solution (mixture of both solutions with normality 10): Number of moles of solute in the resultant solution = 10 x (3.0 + 5.0) According to the problem, the resultant solution has the normality of 10, so we equate the moles of solute in the resultant solution with the calculated value: 10 x (3.0 + 5.0) = N x 3.0 + 4 x 5.0 Now, we solve for N: 10 x 8.0 = N x 3.0 + 20 80 = 3N + 20 3N = 60 N = 20

questions.text: A 0.1097 gm sample of As2O3 required 26.10 mL of KMnO4 solution for its titration. The molarity of KMnO4 solution is:

This option is incorrect because it significantly overestimates the molarity of KMnO4.

questions.explanation: The molarity of KMnO4 solution can be calculated using the formula: Molarity (M) = (Volume of KMnO4 solution in liters) / (Number of moles of As2O3) First, let's calculate the number of moles of As2O3: Given the mass of As2O3 = 0.1097 gm Molar mass of As2O3 = 2 x (Atomic mass of As) + 3 x (Atomic mass of O) = 2 x 74.92 + 3 x 16.00 = 197.84 gm/mol Number of moles of As2O3 = Mass / Molar mass = 0.1097 gm / 197.84 gm/mol ≈ 0.000553 mol Now, let's calculate the molarity of KMnO4: Volume of KMnO4 solution = 26.10 mL = 26.10 cm3 = 26.10 x 10-3 L Molarity (M) = (Volume in liters) / (Number of moles of As2O3) Molarity (M) = 26.10 x 10-3 L / 0.000553 mol ≈ 47.18 M Rounded to three significant figures, the molarity of KMnO4 solution is approximately 0.018 M.

questions.explanation: When magnesium (Mg) reacts with an acid, it typically displaces hydrogen, forming hydrogen gas (H2). The balanced chemical equation for this reaction is:Mg + 2H+ → Mg2+ + H2From this equation, we see that one mole of Mg produces one mole of H2. Since we have 12 g of Mg, which is 0.5 moles (12 g / 24 g/mol), it will produce 0.5 moles of H2 gas. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Half mole of H2'.Options A and D are incorrect because they suggest incorrect stoichiometric relationships, and Option B is incorrect because O2 is not produced in this reaction.

questions.text: When pentane C5H12 is burned in excess oxygen, the products of the reaction are CO2(g) and H2O(l). In the balanced equation for this combustion,C5H12(g) _ O2 (g) → 5CO2(g) + 6H2O(l) the coefficient of the oxygen should be:

options.explanation: Incorrect. While 16 is the total number of oxygen atoms needed, it is not the coefficient of O2. The coefficient must reflect the number of O2 molecules, making this option incorrect.

Incorrect. 11 O2 molecules would provide an incorrect number of oxygen atoms. Verify the calculation by balancing carbon and hydrogen first.
Correct. By balancing carbon and hydrogen atoms first, you find that 8 O2 molecules are needed to provide the 16 oxygen atoms required for complete combustion.

questions.explanation: When hydrocarbon reacts with oxygen it forms carbon dioxide and water.C5​H1​2+O2​→CO2​+H2​OStart by using the 5 carbons in the hydrocarbon to form 5 carbon dioxide.Then use the 12 hydrogens to form 6 waters. Then count up the oxygen atoms needed.5×O2​=10O6×O1​=6O = 16There must be 16 oxygens on the left or reactants to balance the equation. So8×O2​=16OHence,1×C5​H12​+8×O2​→5×CO2​+6H2​OThe balanced equation is:C5​H12​+8O2​→5CO2​+6H2​O

questions.text: When 16.6 g of KI is treated with an excess of KIO3 in the presence of 6NHCl, ICl is produced. The amount of KIO3 consumed and the ICl formed are:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: This option suggests that a larger amount of KIO3 was consumed compared to the actual stoichiometric calculations. Therefore, it is incorrect.
This is the correct answer based on stoichiometric calculations from the balanced equation.
This option underestimates both KIO3 consumed and ICl produced. Therefore, it is incorrect.
This option incorrectly suggests a higher amount of KIO3 than what is calculated based on the limiting reactant principle. Hence, it is incorrect.

questions.explanation: To find the correct amounts of KIO3 consumed and ICl formed, first, the balanced chemical equation must be established. Based on stoichiometry, the moles of KI can be determined from its mass:Molar mass of KI = 166 g/mol, thus:Moles of KI = 16.6 g / 166 g/mol = 0.1 mol.Assuming the balanced reaction shows that 1 mole of KI reacts with 1 mole of KIO3 to produce 3 moles of ICl, the limiting reactant is KI, and we would need only 0.05 mol of KIO3 (0.1 mol KI) to react completely with it. Therefore, the amount of ICl produced would be 0.15 mol (0.1 mol KI produces 0.3 mol ICl). Hence, the correct answer is Option B: 0.05 mol of KIO3 consumed and 0.15 mol of ICl formed. The other options either overestimate or underestimate the amounts based on the stoichiometric relationships established in the balanced equation.

questions.text: Which is a molecular ion?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The ammonium ion (NH4+) is a polyatomic ion, not a molecular ion. It is formed from ammonia (NH3) by the addition of a proton (H+), creating a polyatomic structure composed of nitrogen and hydrogen with covalent bonds.
The hydronium ion (H3O+) is also a polyatomic ion, formed when a proton (H+) is added to a water molecule (H2O). It plays an important role in acid-base chemistry but is not a molecular ion.

The NH3+ ion is a true molecular ion because it is formed by the ionization of a neutral ammonia molecule (NH3) without forming a new covalent bond or adding atoms, making it a cationic molecular ion.
The sulfate ion (SO42-) is a polyatomic ion, consisting of one sulfur atom and four oxygen atoms bonded covalently. It does not qualify as a molecular ion.

questions.explanation: A molecule containing a positive or negative charge is known as a molecular ion. After hiding charge on an ion, if it looks independent individual molecule then it is called a molecular ion. If the positive charge on NH3+ is hidden by thumb then NH3+ is independent molecule of ammonia so it is a cationic molecular ion.

options.explanation: 35.5 g of chlorine gas (Cl2) is equivalent to 0.5 moles of Cl2 molecules, as the molar mass of Cl2 is 71 g/mol, which does not equal 1 mole of particles.

options.text: Hydrogen ions in 1 dm3 of 1 mol dm-3 aqueous sulphuric acid
options.explanation: 1 dm3 of 1 mol dm-3 aqueous sulphuric acid contains 1 mole of H2SO4, which dissociates into 2 moles of H+ ions, resulting in 2 moles of hydrogen ions, not 1 mole.

options.text: Electrons in 1 g of hydrogen gas
options.explanation: 1 g of hydrogen gas (H2) contains approximately 0.5 moles of H2 molecules. Since each H2 molecule has 2 electrons, this totals to 1 mole of electrons (6.022 x 1023 electrons).

options.text: Oxygen atoms in 22.4 dm3 of oxygen gas at STP
options.explanation: 22.4 dm3 of oxygen gas at STP contains 1 mole of O2 molecules. Since each O2 molecule consists of 2 oxygen atoms, this results in 2 moles of oxygen atoms, not 1 mole.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C. 1 g of hydrogen gas (H2) is approximately 0.5 moles, and each H2 molecule has 2 electrons, providing 1 mole of electrons (6.022 x 1023 electrons). Option A is incorrect as 35.5 g of chlorine is 0.5 moles, not 1. Option B is incorrect because 1 dm3 of 1 mol dm-3 H2SO4 provides 2 moles of H+ ions. Option D is incorrect because 22.4 dm3 of O2 gas at STP contains 2 moles of oxygen atoms.

questions.text: The number of moles of CO2 which contain 16 g of oxygen:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: According to the solution, this option is not correct.

The number of moles of CO2 which contain 16 g of oxygen is 0.5 moles. This is because each molecule of CO2 contains 2 oxygen atoms, and 16 g of oxygen is equivalent to 0.5 moles of oxygen. So, 0.5 moles of CO2 will contain 16 g of oxygen. The equation for the formation of CO2 is: C + O2 → CO2 where: C is carbon, O2 is oxygen gas and CO2 is carbon dioxide.The molar mass of CO2 is 44 g/mol, and the molar mass of oxygen is 16 g/mol. So, 0.5 moles of CO2 will contain 16 g of oxygen. Therefore, the number of moles of CO2 which contain 16 g of oxygen is 0.5 moles.

questions.text: The relative atomic mass of oxygen is 16amu. What is the mass of 2 mole of oxygen gas?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: This is correct. The molar mass of oxygen gas (O2) is 32 g/mol. Therefore, 2 moles of oxygen gas weigh 64 grams.
This is incorrect. The calculation mistakenly uses a molar mass higher than the actual 32 g/mol for oxygen gas (O2).
This is incorrect. 32 grams is the mass of 1 mole of oxygen gas (O2), not 2 moles.
This is incorrect. The calculation mistakenly uses a molar mass higher than 32 g/mol for oxygen gas (O2).

questions.explanation: The relative atomic mass of oxygen is 16 amu, which means that one mole of oxygen atoms has a mass of 16 grams. However, since we are dealing with oxygen gas (O2) in this question, we need to use the molar mass of oxygen gas to calculate the mass of 2 moles of oxygen gas.The molar mass of oxygen gas is approximately 32 grams per mole (2 x 16 g/mol), so the mass of 2 moles of oxygen gas would be:2 moles x 32 g/mol = 64 gramsTherefore, the mass of 2 moles of oxygen gas is 64 grams.

questions.text: Which of the following is not true for a mole?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because a mole is indeed a counting unit used to quantify the amount of substance, much like a dozen is used to count twelve items.

options.text: It represents the gram atomic or gram molecular mass of a substance
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A mole is defined as the quantity of substance that contains the same number of entities as there are atoms in 12 grams of carbon-12, which corresponds to the substance's gram atomic or gram molecular mass.
This option is incorrect because a mole of any substance indeed contains Avogadro's number of particles, approximately 6.022 x 1023 particles.

options.text: It contains different numbers of particles for different substances
options.explanation: This option is correct because the statement is false. A mole always contains the same number of particles (Avogadro's number) for any substance, although the mass of a mole differs based on the substance's atomic or molecular weight.

questions.explanation: Option d is correct because moles represent a quantity of substance, and this quantity will differ for different substances due to their varying atomic and molecular weights.

Molecular Formula: H2OMolecular Weight: (2 * 1.01) + 16.00 = 18.02 g/molOxygen Weight: 16.00 g/molPercentage of Oxygen by Weight: (16.00 / 18.02) * 100 ≈ 88.88% Hence, this option is correct.

questions.text: Indicate the incorrect statement from the following:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.text: A limiting reactant is consumed at the end of a reaction.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. A limiting reactant is completely consumed in a chemical reaction, determining the maximum amount of product formed.

options.text: Theoretical yield is always lower than actual yield.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Theoretical yield is the maximum amount of product that can be obtained from a reaction, assuming perfect conditions. Actual yield is often lower due to various practical losses.

options.text: Stoichiometric calculations can only be done if no side reaction happens.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. as it is the general rule and requirement for the calculation

options.text: The empirical formula and molecular formula of some compounds are the same.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. For some compounds, such as H2O, both the empirical and molecular formulas are identical.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B. Theoretical yield is the calculated maximum amount of product that can be produced in a chemical reaction under ideal conditions, whereas actual yield is the measured amount obtained from the experiment, which is often less due to factors such as incomplete reactions or losses during recovery. Options A, C, and D are correct statements: a limiting reactant is entirely consumed, stoichiometric calculations can be performed despite side reactions, and some compounds indeed have the same empirical and molecular formulas.

This option is correct because the efficiency of a chemical reaction is determined by the amount of product formed compared to the theoretical yield.

questions.text: The mass of an atom (element) compared with the mass of one atom of C-12 is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The term 'one mole' refers to a quantity of particles, specifically 6.022 x 1023 particles, and not a comparison of masses relative to C-12.
'Gram atomic mass' is an outdated term for the mass of one mole of an element in grams, which is equivalent to the molar mass. It does not describe a mass comparison with C-12.
The atomic number represents the number of protons in an atom's nucleus and does not involve mass comparison to C-12. It is a unique identifier for elements.
The mass of an atom of an element compared with the mass of one atom of C-12 is termed the 'relative atomic mass.' It provides a comparative measure of an element's atomic mass relative to C-12.

questions.explanation: The mass of an atom of an element compared with the mass of one atom of carbon-12 (C-12) is called the relative atomic mass because it provides a relative measure of the mass of an atom or element with respect to Carbon-12.

questions.text: A polymer of empirical formula CH2 has a molar mass of 28000gmol-I. What is its molecular formula?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The molar mass of (CH2)1400 is calculated as (12 + 2) x 1400 = 19600 g/mol. Since this does not equal 28000 g/mol, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: The most doubtful yield is:

options.explanation: The expected yield is calculated based on the stoichiometry of the reaction. It represents the maximum amount of product that can be formed under ideal conditions, without any practical hindrances.

The actual yield is the amount of product obtained from a real-life chemical reaction. It is the most doubtful because it is influenced by various factors like experimental errors, incomplete reactions, and impurities in reactants.

This is a theoretical calculation based on the balanced chemical equation. It assumes that the reaction goes to completion and all limiting reactants are converted to products, which does not consider practical deviations.

The theoretical yield is the calculated maximum product possible from a given amount of reactants, based on stoichiometry. It assumes perfect conditions and complete conversion, making it a more stable concept than actual yield.

questions.explanation: The most doubtful yield is the actual yield. This is because the actual yield is the amount of product that is actually produced in a chemical reaction, and it can be affected by a number of factors, such as impurities in the reactants, experimental errors, and incomplete reaction.

questions.text: How many atoms of carbon are present in 18g of glucose?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect value.
This is Avogadros number.
It should be 1023

The molecular formula of glucose is C6H12O6, which means that there are 6 carbon atoms in every molecule of glucose. The molar mass of glucose is 180 g/mol, so 18 g of glucose contains 18/180 = 0.1 moles of glucose. Since there are 6 carbon atoms in every mole of glucose, there are 0.1 X 6 = 0.6 moles of carbon atoms in 18 g of glucose. Avogadro's number is the number of atoms in a mole of a substance, and it is equal to 6.022 x 1023 atoms/mol. So, there are 0.6 X 6.022 x 1023= 3.613 x 1023 carbon atoms in 18 g of glucose. Therefore, there are 3.613 x 1023carbon atoms present in 18 g of glucose.

questions.text: Stoichiometric calculations are not possible when:

options.text: All the reactants are completely converted into products
options.explanation: This scenario allows for straightforward stoichiometric calculations because the reaction has reached completion without any external factors affecting the outcome.
Stoichiometric calculations remain valid when there are no reversible reactions, as the reaction proceeds in a single direction, ensuring predictable outcomes.

options.text: In calculations, the law of conservation of mass and law of definite proportions are obeyed
options.explanation: These laws are fundamental to stoichiometry, ensuring that reactants and products are balanced, allowing for accurate calculations.
Side reactions introduce unexpected products or consume reactants, disrupting the stoichiometric balance and complicating accurate calculations.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. Side reactions occur alongside the main reaction and can alter the expected outcomes by producing additional products or consuming reactants not anticipated by the primary balanced chemical equation. This disrupts the stoichiometric calculations as the actual chemical species' amounts differ from those predicted.Option A is incorrect because when all reactants are converted into products completely, stoichiometric calculations are straightforward and precise.Option B is incorrect as the absence of reversible reactions allows the reaction to proceed in one direction, simplifying stoichiometry.Option C is incorrect because adherence to the laws of conservation of mass and definite proportions ensures accurate stoichiometric calculations.

questions.text: How many moles of CO2 are produced from 0.9 moles of O2 in the reaction C + O2 -> CO2:

options.explanation: If 0.9 moles of O2 react, we need to determine the stoichiometric ratio between O2 and CO2 in the balanced equation. From the balanced equation, we can see that the coefficient of O2 is 1, and the coefficient of CO2 is also 1. This means that for every mole of O2 reacted, we will produce an equal number of moles of CO2. Therefore, the answer is 0.9 mole of CO2.

questions.text: A limiting reactant is the one:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.text: Which is present in maximum amount
options.explanation: This option suggests that the limiting reactant is the one present in the maximum amount. However, this is not true. The limiting reactant is the reactant that is entirely consumed or "limits" the amount of product that can be formed in a chemical reaction. The limiting reactant is not determined by the initial quantities of reactants but rather by their stoichiometric ratios. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Which produces minimum no. of moles of product
options.explanation: This option accurately describes the concept of a limiting reactant. In a chemical reaction, the stoichiometry of the balanced equation determines the theoretical amount of product that can be formed from each reactant. The reactant that produces the minimum number of moles of product is the limiting reactant because it determines the maximum amount of product that can be obtained. Once the limiting reactant is completely consumed, the reaction stops and no further product can be formed. Therefore, this option is correct.

options.text: Which produces maximum no. of moles of product
options.explanation: The reactant that produces the maximum number of moles of product is not the limiting reactant but rather the reactant in excess. The reactant in excess is not entirely consumed and remains present after the reaction is complete. The limiting reactant is the one that determines the extent of the reaction and sets the maximum amount of product that can be obtained. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Does not affect the amount of product
options.explanation: This option is also incorrect. The limiting reactant directly affects the amount of product that can be formed. As mentioned earlier, the limiting reactant determines the maximum amount of product that can be obtained. If one of the reactants is limiting, it will restrict the reaction's progress and limit the amount of product that can be produced. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: To summarize, the correct answer is option B) "Which produces the minimum number of moles of product". It accurately describes the concept of a limiting reactant. In a chemical reaction, the stoichiometry of the balanced equation determines the theoretical amount of product that can be formed from each reactant. The reactant that produces the minimum number of moles of product is the limiting reactant because it determines the maximum amount of product that can be obtained. Once the limiting reactant is completely consumed, the reaction stops and no further product can be formed.

questions.explanation: The relative atomic mass unit is based on the mass of carbon-12. If we change the basis to 1/6th of the carbon atom's mass instead of 1/12th, the unit becomes larger. Consequently, the number of these larger units in a mole decreases, effectively doubling the mass of one mole of a substance. Therefore, the mass of a mole would increase two-fold. Option C is incorrect because it underestimates the number of protons, and Option D is incorrect because it overestimates the number of protons per molecule.

questions.explanation: Stoichiometry is a branch of chemistry that deals with the quantitative relationships and calculations involving the amounts of reactants and products in chemical reactions. questions.text: Which series of organic compounds shares the same empirical formula:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Refer to the explanation below.
Refer to the explanation below.
Refer to the explanation below.

The series of alkenes share the same empirical formula because they have a consistent ratio of elements. The empirical formula represents the simplest, most reduced ratio of the different elements present in a compound. In alkenes, the general molecular formula is (CnH2n), where n represents the number of carbon atoms. This formula indicates a fixed ratio of carbon to hydrogen, which is 1:2. As you move through the series of alkenes, such as ethene C2H4 propene C3H6, and butene C4H8, the ratio of carbon to hydrogen remains constant. This consistency in the ratio of elements leads to the alkenes in the series having the same empirical formula, which is CH2 for alkenes in general. It's important to note that while the empirical formula is the same, the molecular formulas of individual alkenes within the series differ based on the specific number of carbon atoms in each molecule.

options.explanation: Water (H2O) is a covalent compound where the empirical formula is the same as the molecular formula.

Sodium chloride (NaCl) does not have a molecular formula in the traditional sense because it is an ionic compound, not a molecular compound. In ionic compounds, the bonding occurs through the transfer of electrons between atoms, leading to the formation of ions. Therefore, a molecular formula cannot be written for ionic compounds like NaCl.
Formaldehyde (HCHO) has both empirical and molecular formulas that are the same, as it is a simple covalent compound.
Methane (CH4) is another example of a covalent compound where the empirical formula is identical to the molecular formula.

questions.text: The concept of limiting reactant is not applicable while:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Stoichiometric calculations involve determining amounts of reactants and products from a balanced chemical equation. Limiting reactants are crucial in these calculations since they determine the maximum amount of product formed. Therefore, the concept of limiting reactant is applicable here.
In reversible reactions, reactants and products exist in equilibrium, meaning no reactant is completely consumed. Thus, the concept of a limiting reactant does not apply as none is fully used up.
The theoretical yield is based on the limiting reactant, which dictates the maximum amount of product that can be formed. Thus, the concept of limiting reactant is essential in these calculations.

The concept of limiting reactant is not applicable in reversible reactions, but it is applicable in stoichiometric calculations and calculating theoretical yield. Therefore, 'All of these' cannot be the correct answer.

questions.text: The volume occupied by 1.4g of N2 at S.T.P is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect as the correct answer is 1.12 dm3 according to the working shown below.

To determine the volume occupied by 1.4g of N2 at standard temperature and pressure (STP), we need to use the ideal gas law equation: PV = nRT Where:P = pressure (in atm)V = volume (in dm3)n = number of moles of gasR = ideal gas constant (0.0821 dm3.atm/mol.K)T = temperature (in Kelvin) At STP, the pressure (P) is 1 atm, and the temperature (T) is 273.15 K (0 degrees Celsius) Now, we need to calculate the number of moles of N2. The molar mass of N2 (nitrogen gas) is:Molar mass of N2 = 2 * molar mass of nitrogen (N) = 2 * 14.01 g/mol = 28.02 g/mol Number of moles of N2 = Mass of N2 / Molar mass of N2Number of moles of N2 = 1.4 g / 28.02 g/mol ≈ 0.05 mol Now, we can use the ideal gas law to find the volume (V) occupied by 0.05 moles of N2 at STP: V = nRT / PV = (0.05 mol) * (0.0821 dm3.atm/mol.K) * (273.15 K) / (1 atm)V ≈ 1.12 dm3

questions.text: A compound possesses 8% sulphur by mass. The least possible molecular mass of the compound is:

options.explanation: Incorrect as according to the calculation shown below the minimum molecular mass of the compound which will contain 8% sulphur by mass is 400g.
Incorrect as according to the calculation shown below the minimum molecular mass of the compound which will contain 8% sulphur by mass is 400g.

For the least possible molecular mass of the compound there must be only 1 sulphur atom in the compound. We know that the molar mass of a sulphur atom is 32g/mol. Thus, we should find the mass of the compound which will have exactly 32g of sulphur in its molecular mass. It is given that the compound contains 8% sulphur by mass. What this means is that in 100g of the compound, we will have 8g of sulphur. Now all we have to do is find the mass of the compound which will have 32g of sulphur. Let us denote the overall mass of the compound as x: Sulphur : Compound 8 : 100 32 : x = (100 x 32)/8 = 400 g Hence, the minimum molecular mass of the compound which will contain 8% sulphur by mass is 400g.

questions.text: The amount of product obtained practically is called
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Expected yield is not a standard term used in chemistry. It is often confused with theoretical yield, which refers to the calculated amount of product based on stoichiometry.
Theoretical yield represents the maximum possible amount of product that could be generated in a reaction, assuming perfect conditions and complete conversion of reactants to products.

Actual yield is the quantity of product that is actually obtained from a chemical reaction when it is carried out in the laboratory, often less than the theoretical yield due to various practical losses.
Fractional yield, often referred to as the percent yield, is the ratio of the actual yield to the theoretical yield, expressed as a percentage. It indicates the efficiency of a reaction.

questions.explanation: Actual yield is the amount of product that is actually formed when a reaction is carried out practically in a laboratory.

questions.text: A compound (60 g) on analysis gave C = 24 g, H = 4 g, O = 32 g. Its empirical formula is:

options.explanation: C2H4O2, would mean that the ratio of the number of moles of carbon to hydrogen to oxygen is 2:4:3, which is not possible.
CH2O2 would mean that the ratio of the number of moles of carbon to hydrogen to oxygen is 2:2:2, which is not possible.
C2H2O2, would mean that the ratio of the number of moles of carbon to hydrogen to oxygen is 2:2:3, which is not possible.

In this case, the mass of carbon is 24 g, the mass of hydrogen is 4 g, and the mass of oxygen is 32 g. The atomic mass of carbon is 12 g/mol, the atomic mass of hydrogen is 1 g/mol, and the atomic mass of oxygen is 16 g/mol. Calculations: Carbon: 24 g / 12 g/mol = 2 mol Hydrogen: 4 g / 1 g/mol = 4 mol Oxygen: 32 g / 16 g/mol = 2 mol The ratio of the number of moles of carbon to hydrogen to oxygen is 2:4:2, which simplifies to CH2O. Therefore, the empirical formula of the compound is CH2O.

Calculations: Carbon: 24 g / 12 g/mol = 2 mol Hydrogen: 4 g / 1 g/mol = 4 mol Oxygen: 32 g / 16 g/mol = 2 mol The ratio of the number of moles of carbon to hydrogen to oxygen is 2:4:2, which simplifies to CH2O. options.text: The percent efficiency of the reaction.

options.text: The theoretical yield of the reaction.
options.explanation: Theoretical yield is the maximum amount of product that can be produced from a chemical reaction. It is calculated based on the stoichiometry of the reaction, which is the number of moles of each reactant and product.
The yield of the reaction is the actual yield divided by the theoretical yield. It is a unitless quantity.

questions.text: Lactose commonly used as a binder in tablets has a molecular weight of 342. What weight of CO2 would be formed when 1/12 mole of this compound is burnt completely?(C12H22O11 + 12O2 12CO2 + 11H2O)
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is not correct because the molar mass of carbon dioxide (CO2) is 44.01 g/mol, not 12 g/mol. The molar mass of CO2 is calculated by adding the atomic masses of carbon (12.01 g/mol) and two oxygen atoms (16.00 g/mol each). Therefore, the weight of CO2 formed would be greater than 12 g.

This option is not correct either. As mentioned earlier, the molar mass of CO2 is 44.01 g/mol. Since 1 mole of lactose (C12H22O11) yields 1 mole of CO2, the weight of CO2 formed would be the same as its molar mass, which is 44.01 g/mol. Therefore, the weight of CO2 formed would not be 4.4 g.

The balanced equation is: C12H22O11 + 12O2 → 12CO2 + 11H2OFrom the balanced equation, we can see that the molar ratio between lactose (C12H22O11) and CO2 is 1:12, meaning that 1 mole of lactose reacts to form 12 moles of CO2.First, let's determine the number of moles of lactose:Molar mass of lactose (C12H22O11) = 12(12.01 g/mol) + 22(1.01 g/mol) + 11(16.00 g/mol) = 342.34 g/molMoles of lactose = 1/12 mole (given)Since the molar ratio between lactose and CO2 is 1:12, we can conclude that 1/12 mole of lactose will produce 12/12 = 1 mole of CO2.Now, let's calculate the weight of CO2: Molar mass of CO2 = 12.01 g/mol (carbon) + 16.00 g/mol (oxygen) + 16.00 g/mol (oxygen) = 44.01 g/molWeight of CO2 = moles of CO2 × molar mass of CO2 = 1 mole × 44.01 g/mol = 44.01 gThus, the weight of carbon dioxide (CO2) formed when 1/12 mole of lactose is completely burnt is 44.01 grams.Therefore, the correct answer is 44 g.

This option is also not correct. The molar mass of CO2 is 44.01 g/mol, not 440 g/mol. Since 1 mole of lactose (C12H22O11) yields 1 mole of CO2, the weight of CO2 formed would be the same as its molar mass, which is 44.01 g/mol. Therefore, the weight of CO2 formed would not be 440 g.

questions.explanation: The balanced equation is: C12H22O11 + 12O2 → 12CO2 + 11H2O From the balanced equation, we can see that the molar ratio between lactose (C12H22O11) and CO2 is 1:12, meaning that 1 mole of lactose reacts to form 12 moles of CO2. First, let's determine the number of moles of lactose: Molar mass of lactose (C12H22O11) = 12(12.01 g/mol) + 22(1.01 g/mol) + 11(16.00 g/mol) = 342.34 g/mol Moles of lactose = 1/12 mole (given) Since the molar ratio between lactose and CO2 is 1:12, we can conclude that 1/12 mole of lactose will produce 12/12 = 1 mole of CO2. Now, let's calculate the weight of CO2: Molar mass of CO2 = 12.01 g/mol (carbon) + 16.00 g/mol (oxygen) + 16.00 g/mol (oxygen) = 44.01 g/mol Weight of CO2 = moles of CO2 × molar mass of CO2 = 1 mole × 44.01 g/mol = 44.01 g Thus, the weight of carbon dioxide (CO2) formed when 1/12 mole of lactose is completely burnt is 44.01 grams. Therefore, the correct answer is 44 g.

questions.text: Element A (atomic weight 12.01) and element B (atomic weight 16) combine to form a new substance X. If two moles of B combine with one mole of A, then the weight of one mole of X is

questions.explanation: To determine the weight of one mole of substance X, we need to calculate the molar mass of substance X.Given that element A has an atomic weight of 12.01 g/mol and element B has an atomic weight of 16 g/mol, we can calculate the molar mass of substance X using the given mole ratios.Since two moles of B combine with one mole of A to form substance X, the molar mass of X can be calculated as follows:Molar mass of X = (2 moles of B) * (atomic weight of B) + (1 mole of A) * (atomic weight of A)Molar mass of X = (2 mol) * (16 g/mol) + (1 mol) * (12.01 g/mol)Molar mass of X = 32 g + 12.01 gMolar mass of X = 44.01 g/molTherefore, the weight of one mole of substance X is 44.01 grams.

questions.text: 10 g of carbon burns giving 11.2 litres of CO2 at STP. After combustion the amount of unburnt carbon is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: According to solution, this option is not correct.
According to solution, this option is not correct.

We first have to find the moles of CO2. Since the volume of CO2 is given at STP, we can use the molar volume of a gas at STP, which is 22.4 liters per mole. Number of moles of CO2 = Volume of CO2 at STP / Molar volume at STP = 11.2 liters / 22.4 liters per mole = 0.5 moles Now we have to determine moles of carbon burned The balanced chemical equation for the combustion of carbon is: C + O2 → CO2 From the equation, we can see that 1 mole of carbon reacts to produce 1 mole of CO2. Therefore, the number of moles of carbon burned is also 0.5 moles. Now we have to calculate the mass of carbon burned and we can do that by finding the mass of carbon burned using its molar mass, which is approximately 12.01 g/mol. Mass of carbon burned = Number of moles of carbon dioxide * Molar mass of carbon = 0.5 moles * 12.01 g/mol = 6.005 g The initial mass of carbon before combustion was 10 g. To find the amount of unburnt carbon, we subtract the mass of carbon burned from the initial mass: Amount of unburnt carbon = Initial mass of carbon - Mass of carbon burned = 10 g - 6.005 g = 3.995 g Therefore, after combustion, the amount of unburnt carbon is approximately 3.995 grams and if we round it off, its 4g therefore option C is correct.

questions.text: Which among MnO2, Mn2O3, MnO3 and Mn2O7 contains the maximum percentage by weight of combined oxygen?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: MnO2 has a molecular weight of 87.845 g/mol. The percentage of oxygen is approximately 36.38%. This is not the highest among the options.
MnO3 has a higher percentage of oxygen at approximately 46.22%, but it's still not the highest.
Mn2O3 has a molecular weight that results in an oxygen percentage of approximately 30.04%, which is lower than other options.
Mn2O7 has the highest percentage of oxygen at approximately 50.08%, making it the correct choice.

questions.explanation: To determine which compound contains the maximum percentage by weight of combined oxygen, we need to calculate the percentage of oxygen in each compound. MnO2: Molecular weight of MnO2 = (Molar mass of Mn) + 2 * (Molar mass of Oxygen) = (55.845 g/mol) + 2 * (16.00 g/mol) = 55.845 g/mol + 32.00 g/mol = 87.845 g/mol Percentage of oxygen in MnO2 = (2 * Molar mass of Oxygen) / Molecular weight of MnO2 * 100 = (2 * 16.00 g/mol) / 87.845 g/mol * 100 = 32.00 g/mol / 87.845 g/mol * 100 ≈ 36.38% We will apply this method to the rest of the compunds a, dthe percentage of oxygen in them are:Mn2O3: ≈ 30.04% MnO3: ≈ 46.22% Mn2O7: ≈ 50.08% Therefore, among the given compounds, Mn2O7 contains the maximum percentage by weight of combined oxygen, which is approximately 50.08% and hence option D is correct.

questions.text: A sample of AlF3 contains 3.0 X 1024 F- ions. The number of formula units in this sample is:

options.explanation: According to solution, this option is not correct.

The formula of AlF3 suggests that there are three fluoride ions (F-) for each aluminum ion (Al3+). Given that there are 3.0 x 1024 F- ions, we can calculate the number of formula units of AlF3 as follows: Number of formula units = (Number of F- ions) / (Number of F- ions per formula unit of AlF3) Number of formula units = (3.0 x 10^24) / 3 Number of formula units = 1.0 x 10^24 So, there are 1.0 x 1024 formula units of AlF3 in the given sample. Hence option D is correct.

questions.text: Chlorine CL relative atomic mass is 35.5 this half number is due to
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The half number in the relative atomic mass of Chlorine (35.5) arises from the existence of two naturally occurring isotopes, Chlorine-35 and Chlorine-37. The atomic mass is a weighted average based on the abundance of these isotopes.
The concept of 'half a proton' is not scientifically valid. Atomic masses are not influenced by fractional protons, but rather by the weighted average of isotopic masses.
Similar to protons, neutrons do not exist in halves. The atomic mass of an element is determined by whole numbers of protons and neutrons in its isotopes, not fractions.
Electrons have negligible mass compared to protons and neutrons, and do not contribute to the atomic mass in a way that would result in a half number.

questions.explanation: The Half number in relative atomic mass of Chlorine (i.e 35.5) is due to two naturally occurring isotopes (Chlorine-35 and Chlorine-37).Chlorine-35 is more abundant isotope(accounting for 75.77% of naturally occurring Chlorine) and it’s Atomic Mass is 34.97 amu.Chlorine-37 is less abundant isotope(accounting for remaining 24.33% of naturally occurring Chlorine) and it’s Atomic Mass is 36.97 amu.Average atomic mass=(Abundance of Chlorine-35 x Atomic mass of Chlorine-35) + (Abundance of Chlorine-37 x Atomic Mass of Chlorine-37)(0.7577 x 34.97 amu) + (0.2423 x 36.97 amu)=26.52 amu + 8.96=35.48 amu (Rounded of as 35.5 amu)

questions.text: Which of the following element is used in the treatment of cancer?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Uranium is not typically used in cancer treatment due to its high radioactivity and the potential for harm to healthy tissues. Medical applications prefer isotopes that can target cancer cells more precisely, like iodine-131 or cobalt-60.

Cobalt-60, an isotope of cobalt, is used in radiotherapy for the treatment of cancer. It emits gamma rays that can be targeted at cancerous tissues to destroy them while minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissues.
Nickel is not used in cancer treatment. It lacks the radioactivity necessary for treating cancer and is not suitable for medical applications due to its potential toxicity.
While iodine, particularly the radioactive iodine-131, is used in medical treatments, it is primarily employed for thyroid-related conditions rather than general cancer treatment.

questions.explanation: Isotope of Cobalt is used in treatment of cancer(Rote Learning).
